coredata.dc:description: CuBi2O4 exhibits a high onset potential and theoretically high photocurrent density due to its well-matched bandgap for solar water splitting. However, factors such as low charge carrier mobility and severe charge recombination prevent CuBi2O4 from reaching its ideal photoelectrochemical performance, while photocorrosion of the Cu element severely compromises its stability. Herein, a convenient and low-cost chemical bath deposition method has been employed to combine carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with Ni(OH)2, resulting in the preparation of a conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer-coated CuBi₂O₄/CuO heterojunction photocathode. Under AM 1.5G illumination, CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 illustrates a photocurrent density of −1.36 mA cm−2, which is 2.12 times higher than that of CuBi2O4/CuO. More importantly, the conformal coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2 effectively prevents photocorrosion induced by oxidation-reduction reactions of Cu2+/Cu. At 0.3 VRHE, the photocurrent of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 remains at 76.4% after 10000 s of chopped light illumination, significantly outperforming the 48.9% retention observed for CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode after 3600 s of chopped light illumination. This work demonstrates that the CQDs and Ni(OH)2 are good candidates for the photoelectrode overlayer with significant synergetic effects.

head.ce:title: CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode with conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 coating for stable solar water splitting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CuBi2O4 exhibits a high onset potential and theoretically high photocurrent density due to its well-matched bandgap for solar water splitting. However, factors such as low charge carrier mobility and severe charge recombination prevent CuBi2O4 from reaching its ideal photoelectrochemical performance, while photocorrosion of the Cu element severely compromises its stability. Herein, a convenient and low-cost chemical bath deposition method has been employed to combine carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with Ni(OH)2, resulting in the preparation of a conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer-coated CuBi₂O₄/CuO heterojunction photocathode. Under AM 1.5G illumination, CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 illustrates a photocurrent density of −1.36 mA cm−2, which is 2.12 times higher than that of CuBi2O4/CuO. More importantly, the conformal coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2 effectively prevents photocorrosion induced by oxidation-reduction reactions of Cu2+/Cu. At 0.3 VRHE, the photocurrent of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 remains at 76.4% after 10000 s of chopped light illumination, significantly outperforming the 48.9% retention observed for CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode after 3600 s of chopped light illumination. This work demonstrates that the CQDs and Ni(OH)2 are good candidates for the photoelectrode overlayer with significant synergetic effects.

ce:section.ce:para: Green hydrogen is a crucial and clean energy source that is urgently needed to construct a future energy society. Solar water splitting stands out as one of the pivotal pathways to produce green hydrogen. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is an important strategy for converting solar energy into hydrogen energy. For the PEC devices, photocathodes are composed of p-type semiconductor films, which are capable of generating electrons under light illumination and reducing the water into hydrogen on the surface of the photocathodes. Many p-type semiconductors are expensive, toxic, and made of rare elements such as p-Si [1,2], CdTe [3], InP [4], and GaAs [5]. Hence, it is imperative to pursue low-cost and abundant p-type materials, such as Cu-based semiconductors [6]. Copper bismuth oxide (CuBi2O4) has a bandgap of 1.5–1.8 eV, remarkable light absorption, and a theoretical photocurrent density of up to 29 mA cm−2 [7]. With a flat band potential of approximately 1.2 VRHE, it exhibits impressive photovoltage and a significant positive onset potential. However, there are several challenges, such as low charge transfer and severe charge recombination, that hinder the PEC performance of CuBi2O4 photocathodes [6,8–10]. In order to suppress the charge recombination, some heterojunctions, such as CuBi2O4/CuO [11,12], CuBi2O4/In2O3 [13], and CuBi2O4/CdO [14], have been explored to enhance charge separation.

The poor stability of Cu-based semiconductors is a more severe issue that impedes their practical applications. The oxidation-reduction potential of Cu2+/Cu lies within the range of water splitting. This results in the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ and subsequent dissolution of Cu element into the electrolyte during water splitting. This phenomenon leads to electrode thinning and performance degradation, which is referred to as photocorrosion [15–17]. Extensive efforts have been made to achieve better performance and stability in CuBi2O4 photocathodes. Materials such as ZnO [12], C [18],TiO2 [16,19,20], and other Ti-based oxides [21,22] have been proven in previous studies to effectively enhance the stability of CuBi2O4 photocathodes. A conformal TiO2 passivation layer-coated CuBi2O4 obtains a photocurrent density of −0.3 mA cm−2 and a photocurrent retention of 88.8% after a 6 h stability test [16]. CuBi2O4 photocathodes with amorphous TiO2 and Pt loading show a photocurrent density of −0.35 mA cm−2 and a 60 min stability with a retention rate of ∼100% [19]. In our previous research, an amorphous ZnO layer coated CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode achieved 84% photocurrent retention after a 12 h stability test in the electrolyte with the sacrificial reagent of H2O2 [12]. The aforementioned studies indicate that enhancing the stability of CuBi2O4 photocathodes by suppressing copper element corrosion through surface coating is a feasible approach. However, the PEC performance and stability of CuBi2O4 photocathodes still remain poor. It's urgent to explore new coating materials to address the above challenges.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer excellent conductivity, a simple preparation method, and low-cost [23,24]. Semiconductive CQDs can also generate electrons and holes upon light irradiation. It has been confirmed that CQDs can reduce the charge transfer resistance and accelerate charge transfer in photoelectrodes like TiNbO/Bi2S3/CQDs [25], BiVO4/CQDs/FeOOH/NiOOH [26], and C–BiVO4/CQDs [27]. These characteristics render CQDs as promising materials for improving the PEC performances of CuBi2O4 photocathodes. In addition, Ni(OH)2 normally exhibits strong hydrogen evolution activity and serves as a cocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Ni(OH)2 can promote the HER reaction by adsorbing OH−, and suppress the surface charge recombination [28]. Therefore, it's expected that incorporating CQDs and Ni(OH)2 onto the surface of CuBi2O4-based photocathodes will enhance their photocurrent and PEC stability.

In this work, we successfully prepared CuBi2O4/CuO photocathodes with conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 coating using a chemical bath deposition method. The composite photocathode of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 exhibits a photocurrent density 2.12 times higher than that of the uncoated CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode. In an electrolyte without a sacrificial agent, the photocurrent density of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 remains at 76.4% after 10000 s of chopped light illumination, far exceeding the 48.9% observed for CuBi2O4/CuO after 3600 s of chopped light illumination. During the cyclic voltammetry measurements, CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 did not exhibit the obvious oxidation-reduction peaks of Cu2+/Cu that were observed in CuBi2O4/CuO. These findings clearly demonstrate that the conformal coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2 can effectively stabilize the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode and improve its PEC performances.

Fabrication of CuBi
2
O
4
/CuO. The fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates were sonicated in acetone, ethanol, and deionized (DI) water sequentially for 30 min, and dried by the N2 blowing. The dried FTO substrates were also placed in a UV ozone cleaner for a surface treatment of 15∼20 min. The electrodeposition of CuBi2O4/CuO was conducted following a previous study [11]. Firstly, concentrated HNO3 was added to a 0.4 M KI solution, and the pH was adjusted to 1.7. Secondly, 0.04 M Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added to the above solution and stirred until completely dissolved. Thirdly, an anhydrous ethanol solution containing 0.23 M p-benzoquinone was prepared. Subsequently, 25 mL KI and Bi(NO3)3 mixed solution and 10 mL p-benzoquinone solution were thoroughly mixed with the stirring vigorously. Electrodeposition was conducted on FTO substrates at room temperature at a constant potential of −0.1 VAg/AgCl for 600 s, followed by rinsing and drying of the electrode to obtain the BiOI electrode. Subsequently, the electrode was immersed in a 0.2 M Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O solution. To fabricate the BiOI/Cu2O electrode, Cu2O was electrodeposited at a constant potential of −0.3 V Ag/AgCl for 300 s Finally, in order to obtain CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode, the electrode was annealed at 450 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1.

Preparation of CQDs solution. 1.2 g of sucrose was added to 40 mL of DI water and placed in a 60 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The hydrothermal process was conducted at 180 °C for 300 min to obtain the precursor solution. The precursor solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and this process was repeated twice. The upper layer solution after the centrifugation was collected to obtain a transparent and brown CQDs solution. The concentration of the obtained CQDs solution is around 20 mg mL−1.

Fabrication of CuBi
2
O
4
/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)
2
. 3 mL of the above CQDs solution was added to 30 mL of 0.05 M Ni(NO3)2·6H2O solution and stirred for 30 min. The as-prepared CuBi2O4/CuO electrode was immersed in the above solution and soaked for 12 h, followed by drying at 70 °C for 6 h. In addition, CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD and CuBi2O4/CuO/Ni(OH)2 electrodes were prepared through immersion in CQDs solution and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O solution for 12 h, respectively.

Characterizations. The prepared electrodes were analyzed for surface morphology and composition using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Scientific Apreo 2C) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Microscopic morphology, interplanar spacing, and crystal plane indexing were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S G2). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV) was utilized to determine the phase composition, structure, and growth orientation of the electrodes. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-ALPHA) provided information on the surface composition and valence states of the electrodes. UV–vis absorption spectra of the electrodes and CQDs solution in the range of 300–900 nm were obtained using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET IS50 FT-IR) was employed to analyze the functional groups on the surface of powder samples. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained using the FLS-980 (Edinburgh Instruments).

PEC measurements. All electrochemical tests were conducted using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) with a three-electrode system. Platinum wire served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and a pH = 9.18 sodium tetraborate buffer solution was used as the electrolyte. During PEC tests, a Xenon lamp was used as the light source, irradiating from the back of the electrode, with light intensity adjusted to 100 mW cm−2 (AM 1.5G). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were taken at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. Mott-Schottky plots were recorded in the dark at a fixed frequency of 2000 Hz. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at 0.3 VRHE under light illumination, with a 10 mV perturbation in the frequency range of 0.1∼105 Hz. EIS fitting was performed using Z-view software. Charge injection and separation efficiencies of all photoelectrodes were tested by adding 1% (v/v) H2O2 to the electrolyte. In addition, LSV and Tafel curves were obtained at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 in the dark in the sodium tetraborate buffer solution. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CQDs solution were obtained using FTO glass as the working electrode in CQDs solution at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.

XRD pattern of the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode is shown in Fig. S1. The tetragonal phase CuBi2O4 (PDF#71–1774) can be identified with the XRD peaks located at 20.9, 28.0, and 37.4°, corresponding to the crystal planes of (200), (211), and (202), respectively. The XRD peaks at 35.5, 38.6, and 48.8° can be attributed to the (−111), (111), and (−202) crystal planes of monoclinic phase CuO (PDF#80-0076), respectively [12]. The XRD result confirms the successful preparation of the CuBi2O4/CuO heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, some CuBi2O4 nanosheets and CuO octahedral structures can be observed at the bottom and top of CuBi2O4/CuO film, respectively. This morphology is consistent with our previous results [12]. After the coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2, no obvious morphology change can be observed at the low-magnification in Fig. 1c. However, at high-magnification, the surface of CuBi2O4/CuO nanostructures has been well covered with CQD@Ni(OH)2 as shown in Fig. 1d. The thickness of CuBi2O4 nanosheets increases from ∼60.00 nm to ∼98.04 nm. In the inset of Fig. 1d, some adhesive-like structures can be considered CQDs and Ni(OH)2. Therefore, the SEM results illustrate that the coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2 is conformal and uniform on the surface of the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode. The EDS pattern in Fig. 1e further confirms the existence of Cu, Bi, O, C, and Ni elements in the CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 composite film. The atomic ratios of various elements are shown in the inset of Fig. 1e. EDS mappings in Fig. S2 demonstrate that the element distribution of Cu, Bi, O, C, and Ni is uniform.

To explore the bonding forms between CQDs and Ni(OH)2, the FTIR spectra of CQDs and CQD@Ni(OH)2 powder were collected, and the results are shown in Fig. 1f. At the wavenumber of 3353 cm−1, there is a characteristic absorption band of –OH in CQDs. The absorption peaks at 2926, 1360, and 1013 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of C–H, CC, and C–O, respectively, indicating that CQDs contain many oxygen-containing functional groups. In contrast to the FTIR spectrum of CQDs, the broad peaks appear at lower wavenumbers, with peaks at 794 and 685 cm−1 corresponding to C–O–Ni and Ni–OH in CQD@Ni(OH)2, respectively. Additionally, the sharp absorption peak appears at 3542 cm−1, which is associated with the –OH stretching vibration of the inorganic compound of β-Ni(OH)2 [29]. This is a direct evidence that can confirm the successful formation of Ni(OH)2. Moreover, the shift of CO, CC, and C–O peaks illustrates that there is some interaction between CQDs and Ni(OH)2. In Fig. 1g, the Ni 2p XPS spectrum of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 exhibits the Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 peaks at 874.4 and 856.2 eV, respectively. This Ni 2p XPS spectrum is well consistent with the reported Ni 2p XPS spectra of Ni(OH)2, indicating the formation of Ni(OH)2 during the chemical bath deposition procedure [30,31].

The properties of the synthesized CQDs were further characterized in detail. In Fig. 2 a, the SEM image of CQDs dispersed on the surface of a silicon wafer indicates the size of CQDs is around 10–20 nm. This size is consistent with the reported results with the same synthesis method [26,27,32]. In Fig. 2b, the as-prepared CQDs solution exhibits a transparent brownish-clear appearance and shows the Tyndall effect under red laser illumination (Inset of Fig. 2c, left image), indicating the colloidal nature of the CQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 2c, the PL emission wavelength of CQDs solution gradually increases with increasing excitation wavelength, indicating the existence of surface defects [32]. Under the illumination with 365 nm UV lamp, the green fluorescence can be clearly observed, which is consistent with the PL emission results (Inset of Fig. 2c, right image) [27,32]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs solution in Fig. 2d displays significant light absorption in the range of 300∼500 nm. Tauc plot of the CQDs solution was calculated and shown in Fig. 2d [33]. A direct bandgap of 2.37 eV can be derived from the Tauc plot. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CQDs is obtained from the CV test in the CQDs solution [32,34]. As shown in Fig. 2e, the distinct oxidation-reduction peaks are observed, with the reduction peak at −0.12 VRHE attributed to the electrochemical reaction of LUMO [27,32,34,35]. Considering the bandgap of CQDs, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of CQDs is determined to be 2.25 VRHE.

The XPS spectra in Fig. 3 a–d were collected to study the changes in surface chemical states after the loading of the CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer. The C 1s spectrum of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 exhibits three deconvoluted peaks, including 284.8 eV for C–C, 286.1 eV for C–O, and 288.0 eV for O–CO groups. Among them, the C–O and O–CO peaks should come from the rich oxygen-containing functional groups such as –OH and –COOH of CQDs. The C 1s spectrum of CuBi2O4/CuO shows convolution of C–C and O–CO groups, attributing to surface carbon contamination. The O 1s XPS spectra can be fitted with three peaks corresponding to the lattice oxygen (OL), oxygen vacancies (OV), and surface hydroxyl (−OH) groups. After the coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2, the intensity of the OV peak increases due to the surface oxygen vacancies in the CQDs [32]. The intensity of the OL peak decreases while the intensity of the –OH peak significantly increases, confirming the successful covering of CQD@Ni(OH)2. In the Bi 4f spectrum of CuBi2O4/CuO, the peaks of 164.3 and 159.0 eV are attributed to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+, respectively [36,37]. The Cu 2p spectrum of CuBi2O4/CuO shows two satellite peaks, and the peaks at 953.6 and 933.7 eV belong to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively. With a binding energy difference of about 20 eV between Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, this spin-orbit splitting indicates the presence of Cu2+ [38]. The Cu 2p3/2 peak can be resolved into components corresponding to both Cu2+ and Cu+. Fig. 3d illustrates that the main oxidation state of Cu is still +2 in CuBi2O4/CuO. The presence of Cu+ in CuBi2O4/CuO should be attributed to the OV defects, leading to the formation of Cu+−OV defect complexes. This observation aligns with the O 1s spectra shown in Fig. 3b. As previous reported, the Cu+−OV defect complexes can be generated at the calcination temperature of <500 °C due to the charge compensation [39,40]. After the coating of CQD@Ni(OH)2, the binding energy of Bi 4f7/2 in CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 shifts from 159.0 eV to 159.3 eV, and the binding energy of Cu 2p3/2 shifts from 933.7 eV to 933.1 eV. This indicates that the valence of Bi and Cu elements can be tailored with the conformal layer of CQD@Ni(OH)2, where the charge transfer between this conformal layer and CuBi2O4/CuO [32,41].

TEM characterizations of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 were further performed. In Fig. 3e, spherical CQDs with a size of ∼13–23 nm can be observed in the zones 1 and 2. In the HRTEM images, typical interplanar spacings of 0.32 and 0.19 nm are observed, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of CQDs, respectively. Additionally, an interplanar spacing of 0.26 nm belongs to the (−111) plane of CuO (Zone 3). The ultrathin layer encapsulating the CQDs and CuBi2O4/CuO is believed to be β-Ni(OH)2, whose crystal plane information can be observed in Fig. 3f. The interplanar spacing of 0.27 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of β-Ni(OH)2, while the spacing of 0.90 nm perhaps belongs to the layer spacing along the [001] orientation [42]. This further proves the successful growth of CQD@Ni(OH)2 on the surface of CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode.

UV–visible absorption spectra could reveal the optical properties of the fabricated electrodes. As shown in Fig. S3, compared to the CuBi2O4/CuO electrode, CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 exhibits higher absorption intensity in the range of 300∼900 nm, particularly noticeable in 300∼500 nm. The CQDs should mainly contribute additional light absorption in this range, which has a bandgap of 2.37 eV. The optical bandgap of the photocathode increases from 1.67 eV to 1.83 eV, indicating that the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 increases the optical bandgap of the composite electrode to a certain extent.

LSV measurements play a crucial role in reflecting the PEC performance of the photoelectrode. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the scan rate was set at 20 mV s⁻1, with testing conducted from 1.1 to 0.3 VRHE under AM 1.5G chopped light. All the photocathodes illustrate a good response when the light is on/off, and an onset potential of ∼1.0 VRHE. According to the reported results of CQDs amount optimization, a deposition time of 12 h was directly used to ensure the complete covering of the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode [27]. It is clearly observed that an obvious dark current appears after loading CQDs and CQD@Ni(OH)2 in Fig. 4a. This should be attributed to the superior electrocatalytic performance of CQDs and Ni(OH)2, which can enhance charge transfer and increase the active sites for hydrogen evolution [43,44]. Moreover, it should be noted that all the photocurrents discussed in the text are obtained by subtracting the dark current at the given potential to reveal the “true” performance of solar water splitting. At 0.37 VRHE, the photocurrent density of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is −1.36 mA cm−2, which is significantly higher than that of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD at −0.93 mA cm−2 and CuBi2O4/CuO at −0.64 mA cm−2. As a control, the photocathode of Ni(OH)2 modified exhibits a high photocurrent density of −1.79 mA cm−2 at the same potential shown in Fig. S4a. When compared to the reported CuBi2O4-based photocathodes (Table S1), this photocurrent of −1.36 mA cm−2 is one of the highest obtained in weakly alkaline and neutral electrolytes without sacrificial reagents. At 0.3 VRHE, the transient photocurrent curves were also collected, as shown in Fig. 4b and S4b. The transient decay time (τ) can be calculated using equation (1).

Where I in, I s, and I t refer to the instantaneous peak photocurrent, steady-state current, and photocurrent at time t, respectively. The time t at which lnD = −1 is defined as the decay time [45,46]. It is evident that the CQD@Ni(OH)2 modified electrode exhibits a longer τ than that of the as-prepared CuBi2O4/CuO. However, only the CQDs modified electrode shows a τ value as short as 1 s. This indirectly demonstrates that Ni(OH)2 can effectively suppress charge recombination. More specifically, the photogenerated electrons can be captured by Ni(OH)2 and participate in the water splitting reaction, while CQDs can induce serious surface charge recombination. As shown in Fig. S4b, the Ni(OH)2 modified electrode illustrates the almost same decay time as that of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 photocathode.

The normalized photovoltage decay curves were used to reveal the charge transfer performance at the surface of the photoelectrode [47]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the CQDs and CQD@Ni(OH)2 modified electrodes show a significantly faster response compared to the as-prepared CuBi2O4/CuO electrodes. In contrast, the Ni(OH)2 modified CuBi2O4/CuO electrode exhibits a slow response in Fig. S4c. This indicates that the CQDs can accelerate the charge transfer, while the Ni(OH)2 can serve as the storage layer of electrons. A larger photovoltage of 0.12 V can be obtained with the CQD@Ni(OH)2 modified electrode (Inset of Fig. 4c). A larger photovoltage indicates that there is a stronger built-in field for charge separation [48,49].

Poor stability has always been a major issue for Cu-based photocathode materials. The sharp decline in PEC performance is due to the oxidation-reduction reaction of Cu element [6]. Therefore, we conducted the CV testing to explore whether such oxidation-reduction reactions exist. In Fig. 4d, it can be observed that CuBi2O4/CuO exhibits significant oxidation-reduction pairs that belong to Cu2+/Cu [48]. This indicates that the surface Cu2+ can be reduced and dissolved in the electrolyte during the water splitting process, which is detrimental to HER. However, for CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2, there are no such oxidation-reduction peaks. This clearly demonstrates that CQD@Ni(OH)2 can effectively hinder direct contact between CuBi2O4/CuO and the electrolyte, suppressing the occurrence of oxidation-reduction reactions of Cu2+/Cu. To verify this idea, we conducted the stability tests on the electrodes under AM 1.5G chopped light at 0.3 VRHE. As shown in Fig. 4e, the photocurrent density of CuBi2O4/CuO rapidly decreases by 51.1% over the 3600 s testing period, while CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 only decreases by 23.6% over 10000 s. As a control, the Ni(OH)2 modified electrode shows a fast decrease of 28.75% within a very short time of 350 s (Fig. S4d). Additionally, we also tested the stability of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 under continuous light illumination. As shown in Fig. S5, the photocurrent density does not significantly decrease after 10000 s. This indicates that pure Ni(OH)2 cannot prevent the decrease in photocurrent caused by photocorrosion (Inset of Fig. S4d). While Ni(OH)2 can tailor the transport of photogenerated electrons and participate in the water reduction reaction, the CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 photocathode achieves superior photocurrent density and better stability compared to the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode through synergy with CQDs. To further reveal the stability of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2, SEM image and XPS spectra of the electrode after the stability test were measured. The surface morphology does not undergo significant changes (Inset of Fig. 4d), indicating the good protective role of CQD@Ni(OH)2. In Fig. S6, the Cu⁺–OV defect complexes can also be observed after the stability test. Considering the photocorrosion mechanism of Cu-based photocathodes, this suggests that the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer can effectively prevent the loss of Cu element. No significant changes were found in the XPS spectra after the stability test.

The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was further measured using the CV technique with scan rates ranging from 20 to 100 mV s−1. The relationship between ECSA and the electric double-layer capacitance (C dl) is shown in equations (2) and (3).
(2)
C dl
= Δ J / Δ E
(3)
ECSA =
C dl
/
C s
Here, ΔE and ΔJ refer to the changes in potential and current density within the scanning range, respectively. Since C s represents the capacitance, and C s values are the same for the same solution, we use C dl to quantify the electrode's ECSA. As shown in Fig. S7, the potential range used is 0.94∼1.04 VRHE. As depicted in Fig. 4f, the C dl of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is 0.117 mF cm−2, which is 3.3 times that of CuBi2O4/CuO (0.035 mF cm−2). Similarly, the C dl of CuBi2O4/CuO/Ni(OH)2 and CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD are shown in Fig. S8. CQDs modified electrode (0.102 mF cm−2) exhibits a greatly higher C dl value compared to the CuBi2O4/CuO electrode. However, the Ni(OH)2 modified electrode (0.04 mF cm−2) exhibits a C dl value similar to that of the CuBi2O4/CuO electrode. This indicates CQDs substantially increase the active sites for electrochemical reactions, thereby improving the electrode's HER performance.

HER activity can be confirmed with the overpotential from the dark LSV curves. Herein, the potential corresponding to a current density of −10 mA cm−2 is defined as the overpotential. As shown in Fig. S9a, the Ni(OH)2 modified electrode shows significantly low overpotential. The overpotential of CuBi2O4/CuO/Ni(OH)2 (0.15 V) is much lower than that of CuBi2O4/CuO (0.28 V). Similarly, the overpotential of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is only 0.16 V. This indicates that Ni(OH)2 can serve as the effective cocatalyst and facilitate faster electron transfer. Additionally, the Tafel slope is crucial for the reaction kinetics of the catalyst, which can be obtained using equation (4).
(4)
η = a + b
log
j
Where η, a, b, and j represent overpotential, constant, Tafel slope, and current density, respectively [50]. From Fig. S9b, it can be observed that the electrodes loaded with conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 exhibit a lower Tafel slope of 139.87 mV dec−1 compared to CuBi2O4/CuO (328.27 mV dec−1). Interestingly, the electrode loaded with only Ni(OH)2 (170.49 mV dec−1) also exhibits a much lower Tafel slope compared to the CuBi2O4/CuO electrode, while the CQDs modified electrode exhibits a larger Tafel slope of 280.9 mV dec−1. This indicates that Ni(OH)2 plays a critical role in the interfacial electron transfer on the surface of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2.

To further investigate the kinetic processes of the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 modified electrode, the charge injection efficiency (η inj) and charge separation efficiency (η sep) of the electrodes were tested and calculated in the electrolyte with the addition of 1% (v/v) H2O2, which reflect the surface charge separation and bulk charge separation of the electrode, respectively [32]. At 0.3 VRHE, the η inj value of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is 36.02%, which is higher than that of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD (29.11%) and CuBi2O4/CuO (24.00%). The η sep of CuBi2O4/CuO is 9.61% at 0.3 VRHE. A much larger η sep of 17.97% can be observed with the CQDs coating. However, after the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)₂ coating, the η sep decreases to 14.35%. These results indicate that CQDs can boost bulk charge separation due to their good conductivity, but can't suppress surface charge recombination. Meanwhile, the Ni(OH)2 layer can effectively store and transfer the photogenerated electrons to water molecules. Therefore, there is a synergistic effect between CQDs and Ni(OH)2.

For the Mott-Schottky plots of CuBi2O4-based photocathodes, the negative slope demonstrates a typical characteristic of p-type semiconductors (Fig. 5 c). The flat band potential of CuBi2O4/CuO is 1.23 VRHE. When the CQDs and CQD@Ni(OH)2 were coated onto the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode, the flat band potential was changed to 1.30 and 1.32 VRHE, respectively. This is due to the fact that the electrode is affected by the more positive HOMO energy level of CQDs, which changes the electrochemical equilibrium at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. As shown in Fig. S10a, the flat band potential of the Ni(OH)2 modified electrode shifts negatively to 1.18 VRHE. This is likely because Ni(OH)₂ can more easily capture electrons, causing a redistribution of the surface potential of the electrode [30]. Charge transfer kinetics can be manifested through EIS measurements under AM 1.5G illumination, as shown in Fig. 5d. The inset of Fig. 5d illustrates the equivalent circuit for EIS fitting, which includes series resistance (R s), bulk resistance (R bulk), bulk constant phase element (CPE bulk), charge transfer resistance (R ct), and charge transfer CPE (CPE ct). The fitted element values are summarized in Table 1 . At the measured potential of 0.3 VRHE, the R bulk of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is 1023.00 Ω, which is smaller than that of CuBi2O4/CuO (1445.00 Ω). Moreover, its R ct value is 8335.00 Ω, which is far less than that of CuBi2O4/CuO (27945.00 Ω). As a control in Fig. S10b, CuBi2O4/CuO/Ni(OH)2 exhibits the significantly larger R bulk (2081.00 Ω) and R ct (18441.00 Ω), suggesting the low conductivity of Ni(OH)2. For the CPE ct values, the CQDs and Ni(OH)2 coating can obviously improve the CPE ct. This indicates that the photogenerated electrons can be effectively stored and transferred to water molecules. Therefore, the main roles of CQD@Ni(OH)2 are reducing the charge transfer resistance and enhancing the interfacial capacitance.

The PEC enhancement mechanism of CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 is schematically illustrated in Fig. 6 . According to our previous work, a type-II heterojunction has been formed by the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode [12]. Considering the HOMO and LUMO levels of CQDs, there is a stepwise band structure of CuBi2O4/CuO with the CQDs coating, while Ni(OH)2 serves as a cocatalyst [25]. The Ni2+ ions in the Ni(OH)2 layer can be reduced by photogenerated electrons and form Ni0, which promotes proton reduction to generate H2 and is itself oxidized to form Ni2+ [30]. Within the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer, CQDs rapidly transfer photogenerated electrons to the surface of Ni(OH)2, greatly suppressing charge recombination. Importantly, since CuBi2O4/CuO electrodes are directly exposed to the electrolyte, photocorrosion resulting from redox reactions cannot be avoided. However, the conformal CQD@Ni(OH)2 layer can act as a protective layer to prevent Cu element loss into the electrolyte, providing significant assistance in enhancing PEC stability.

In summary, the CuBi2O4/CuO/CQD@Ni(OH)2 electrode has been successfully prepared using a chemical bath deposition method. CQDs and Ni(OH)2 jointly promote the PEC performance of the CuBi2O4/CuO photocathode. The CQDs with excellent conductivity not only bring additional light absorption but also accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electrons from CuBi2O4/CuO to the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, suppressing charge recombination in the bulk. Meanwhile, the outer layer of highly HER-active Ni(OH)2 participates in the reaction by adsorbing protons from the electrolyte, further inhibiting surface charge recombination. Through this mutual synergy, the electrode exhibits a photocurrent density 2.12 times higher than the original electrode. Moreover, CQD@Ni(OH)2 can be uniformly and conformally loaded on the surface of CuBi2O4/CuO electrodes, isolating direct contact between the electrolyte and Cu2+ ions in CuBi2O4/CuO, directly alleviating the stability degradation caused by photocorrosion. This work demonstrates that the CQDs and Ni(OH)2 are good candidates for the photoelectrode overlayer with significant synergetic effects.
